observations: ~4.5 years of daily-resolution history (2019-01 to mid-2023) with a clear repeating annual cycle: troughs around mid-year (~80-95) and broad peaks around winter (~130-150).
High-frequency oscillation superimposed throughout, consistent with a short (weekly-like) seasonal ripple.
Multiple short, abrupt rectangular bumps that spike well above the local baseline and then snap back: e.g. early 2019 (~155), late 2019 (~135), mid-2020 (~130), mid-2021 (~170), and late 2022 (~135).
The largest excursion occurs at the very start of 2023, jumping to ~200 before dropping back down to ~150.
These spikes have flat-ish tops and steep edges, suggesting on/off temporary events rather than smooth peaks.
Near the forecast origin (mid-2023) the series sits around 140-150 on the descending side of a winter peak, after a temporary plateau, and is trending downward toward ~133 at the very end.
visual.pattern_summary: A strong annual seasonal cycle with ripple, repeatedly interrupted by short temporary level-up events that spike and then revert to the seasonal baseline.
hypotheses: If the recent window happens to include one of the large temporary spikes (e.g. the early-2023 jump to ~200), a recent-window forecast could carry that elevated level forward and over-predict.
The window ends on the descending limb just after a winter peak, so extrapolating recent slope could over- or under-shoot the upcoming seasonal trough.
A short recent window may miss the full annual cycle and fail to anticipate the mid-year seasonal decline.
Treating a transient event-driven level as the new baseline could produce a persistent bias until the event ends.
uncertainties: Whether any spike is a genuine permanent level shift or a temporary event that reverts cannot be confirmed from the tail alone.
Exact dates, durations, and recurrence schedule of the temporary events.
Whether the events are exogenously driven (e.g. promotions/holidays) versus intrinsic to the series.
The true sampling frequency and the precise period of the high-frequency ripple.
Whether another event is imminent just past the forecast origin.

proposal.visual_first_rationale: Visually, the chart shows multiple short, abrupt rectangular bumps with flat tops and steep edges that spike well above the local seasonal baseline and then snap back sharply — e.g. the early-2019 spike to ~155, the late-2019 spike to ~135, the mid-2020 spike to ~130, and most prominently the early-2023 jump to ~200 before dropping back to ~150. These rectangular on/off shapes are the hallmark of temporary events, not smooth ramps or permanent level shifts. The series always returns to its underlying annual seasonal cycle after each spike, confirming these are transient disturbances. Numerically, five candidate event blocks are identified (all closed before the forecast origin), with mean_excess values ranging from ~28 to ~59 — large enough to badly distort trend estimation if left in the training data. The transient_event_score (0.043) appears low because the permanent_shift_magnitude (28.2) is inflated by the model absorbing these temporary spikes as trend. The recurring_event_summary shows is_calendar_recurring=false with confidence only 0.20, ruling out the holiday-tuning tool. The full_history baseline (MAE 37.75) already beats all recent-window variants, confirming the long seasonal history is valuable — we just need to clean the distorting temporary blocks so the model learns the true seasonal baseline rather than an elevated trend contaminated by the spikes.
proposal.expected_effect: Interpolating over the five closed temporary event blocks will remove the artificial upward bias those spikes introduce into trend and level estimation. The model will then fit the true underlying annual seasonal cycle more accurately, reducing over-prediction in the forecast horizon and improving MAE/RMSE below the naive full_history baseline of 37.75/38.49.
